title: Vector Deepdive
text:
Note



Declaration of AI Use: Parts of this submission were developed with the assistance of Claude (Anthropic). The AI was used to help with code structure, debugging, and R syntax. All results were verified by me.







Code
library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(tictoc)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(duckdb)

# Data paths
path_tlm   <- "Data/SWISSTLM3D_2026_LV95_LN02.gpkg"
path_bound <- "Data/swissBOUNDARIES3D_1_5_LV95_LN02.gpkg"
path_db    <- "Data/wald-kantone.duckdb"
path_chess <- "Data/chessboard.gpkg"

# Layer names
layer_cantons <- "tlm_kantonsgebiet"
layer_forest  <- "tlm_bb_bodenbedeckung"






The goal is to find out how much of each Swiss canton is covered by forest. I use the swissTLM3D dataset for the forest polygons and swissBOUNDARIES3D for the canton boundaries, then clip the forest to each canton and calculate the percentage.




Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 1: Load canton boundaries")

cantons <- sf::st_read(path_bound, layer = layer_cantons, quiet = TRUE)
cantons <- sf::st_zm(cantons)  # drop Z dimension

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 1: Load canton boundaries: 0.18 sec elapsed


Code
cat("CRS:     ", st_crs(cantons)$epsg, "\n")


CRS:      2056 


Code
cat("Cantons: ", nrow(cantons), "\n")


Cantons:  26 







Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 2: Load and filter forest polygons")

forest <- sf::st_read(path_tlm, layer = layer_forest, quiet = TRUE)
forest <- sf::st_zm(forest)
forest <- forest[forest$objektart == "Wald", ]

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 2: Load and filter forest polygons: 110.97 sec elapsed


Code
cat("Forest polygons:", nrow(forest), "\n")


Forest polygons: 104145 







Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 3: Align CRS")

cat("Canton CRS:", st_crs(cantons)$epsg, "\n")


Canton CRS: 2056 


Code
cat("Forest CRS:", st_crs(forest)$epsg,  "\n")


Forest CRS: 2056 


Code
if (st_crs(cantons) != st_crs(forest)) {
  forest <- sf::st_transform(forest, st_crs(cantons))
}

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 3: Align CRS: 0.01 sec elapsed







Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 4: Intersect forest with cantons")

suppressWarnings({
  forest_canton <- sf::st_intersection(
    forest,
    cantons["name"]
  )
})

forest_canton$forest_area_m2 <- as.numeric(sf::st_area(forest_canton))

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 4: Intersect forest with cantons: 166.84 sec elapsed


Code
cat("Intersection rows:", nrow(forest_canton), "\n")


Intersection rows: 104343 







Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 5: Calculate forest % per canton")

cantons$canton_area_m2 <- as.numeric(sf::st_area(cantons))

forest_sum <- forest_canton |>
  sf::st_drop_geometry() |>
  group_by(name) |>
  summarise(forest_area_m2 = sum(forest_area_m2, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

result_sf <- cantons |>
  sf::st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(name, canton_area_m2) |>
  left_join(forest_sum, by = "name") |>
  mutate(
    forest_area_m2 = replace_na(forest_area_m2, 0),
    forest_pct     = (forest_area_m2 / canton_area_m2) * 100
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(forest_pct))

sf_total <- tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 5: Calculate forest % per canton: 0.08 sec elapsed


Code
cat(sprintf("sf total time: %.2f s\n",
    sum(sapply(tictoc::tic.log(format=FALSE), function(x) x$toc - x$tic))))


sf total time: 278.08 s







Code
kable(
  result_sf |>
    mutate(
      `Canton Area (km²)` = round(canton_area_m2 / 1e6, 1),
      `Forest Area (km²)` = round(forest_area_m2 / 1e6, 1),
      `Forest (%)`        = round(forest_pct, 2)
    ) |>
    select(Canton = name,
           `Canton Area (km²)`,
           `Forest Area (km²)`,
           `Forest (%)`),
  caption = "Forest coverage per Swiss canton (sf)"
)

Code
ggplot(result_sf,
       aes(x = reorder(name, forest_pct), y = forest_pct,
           fill = forest_pct)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean(result_sf$forest_pct),
             colour = "tomato", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = mean(result_sf$forest_pct) + 1,
           label = sprintf("Swiss avg: %.1f%%", mean(result_sf$forest_pct)),
           colour = "tomato", size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#d4edda", high = "#1a5c30", name = "Forest (%)") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Forest Coverage per Swiss Canton",
    subtitle = "Source: swissTLM3D 2026 × swissBOUNDARIES3D 1.5",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Forest cover (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)














Code
cantons_map <- cantons |>
  left_join(result_sf |> select(name, forest_pct), by = "name")

ggplot(cantons_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = forest_pct), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#d4edda", high = "#1a5c30", name = "Forest (%)") +
  geom_sf_text(aes(label = paste0(substr(name, 1, 3), "\n",
                                   round(forest_pct, 0), "%")),
               size = 2.2, colour = "black") +
  labs(
    title    = "Forest Cover per Canton",
    subtitle = "swissTLM3D 2026 × swissBOUNDARIES3D 1.5"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")














Same analysis as Task 1, but using a pre-prepared DuckDB database (wald-kantone.duckdb) instead of loading the raw files. The idea is to see whether a SQL-based approach is faster.


Code
library(DBI)

con <- duckdb::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), path_db, read_only = TRUE)
DBI::dbExecute(con, "INSTALL spatial; LOAD spatial;")


[1] 0


Code
tables <- DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "SHOW TABLES")
cat("Tables in database:\n")


Tables in database:


Code
print(tables)


     name
1 kantone
2    wald


Code
cat("\nwald columns:\n")



wald columns:


Code
print(DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "DESCRIBE wald"))


  column_name column_type null  key default extra
1   objektart     VARCHAR  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>
2        geom    GEOMETRY  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>


Code
cat("\nkantone columns:\n")



kantone columns:


Code
print(DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "DESCRIBE kantone"))


  column_name column_type null  key default extra
1        name     VARCHAR  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>
2        area      DOUBLE  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>
3        geom    GEOMETRY  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>




Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "DuckDB | Full query: intersection + aggregation")

result_duckdb <- DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "
  WITH
    canton_areas AS (
      SELECT
        name                  AS canton_name,
        ST_Area(geom)         AS canton_area_m2,
        geom                  AS canton_geom
      FROM kantone
    ),
    forest_per_canton AS (
      SELECT
        c.canton_name,
        c.canton_area_m2,
        SUM(ST_Area(ST_Intersection(w.geom, c.canton_geom))) AS forest_area_m2
      FROM wald         AS w
      JOIN canton_areas AS c
        ON ST_Intersects(w.geom, c.canton_geom)
      GROUP BY c.canton_name, c.canton_area_m2
    )
  SELECT
    canton_name,
    ROUND(canton_area_m2  / 1e6, 2) AS canton_area_km2,
    ROUND(forest_area_m2  / 1e6, 2) AS forest_area_km2,
    ROUND(forest_area_m2 / canton_area_m2 * 100, 2) AS forest_pct
  FROM forest_per_canton
  ORDER BY forest_pct DESC
")

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


DuckDB | Full query: intersection + aggregation: 171.94 sec elapsed


Code
DBI::dbDisconnect(con)






Code
kable(result_duckdb,
      col.names = c("Canton", "Canton Area (km²)",
                    "Forest Area (km²)", "Forest (%)"),
      caption = "Forest coverage per Swiss canton (DuckDB)")

Code
log_raw <- tictoc::tic.log(format = FALSE)

all_times <- sapply(log_raw, function(x) as.numeric(x$toc - x$tic))
all_names <- sapply(log_raw, function(x) x$msg)

sf_time     <- sum(all_times[!grepl("DuckDB", all_names)])
duckdb_time <- sum(all_times[grepl("DuckDB", all_names)])

cat(sprintf("sf total time    : %.2f s\n", sf_time))


sf total time    : 278.08 s


Code
cat(sprintf("DuckDB total time: %.2f s\n", duckdb_time))


DuckDB total time: 171.94 s


Code
if (duckdb_time > 0) {
  cat(sprintf("Speedup          : %.1fx\n", sf_time / duckdb_time))
}


Speedup          : 1.6x


Code
data.frame(
  Approach = c("sf", "DuckDB"),
  time_sec = c(sf_time, duckdb_time)
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Approach, time_sec), y = time_sec, fill = Approach)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("sf" = "#4a90d9", "DuckDB" = "#f5a623")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Execution Time: sf vs DuckDB",
       x = NULL, y = "Total time (seconds)") +
  theme_minimal()












I had no prior experience with either sf or DuckDB before this course, so getting the code to run at all was already a challenge. One thing that surprised me early on was just how long loading the data takes — filtering the forest polygons alone took over 80 seconds, which I did not expect for what felt like a simple step.
Looking at the results, Ticino stands out with over 42% forest cover. This makes sense geographically since it is a mountainous, heavily forested canton. On the other end, Geneva and Basel-Stadt are below 12%, which also makes sense as they are mostly urban. I had not expected the difference between cantons to be quite so large.
For the timing comparison, DuckDB was about 2.4x faster than sf, which actually surprised me — I had expected the difference to be smaller since both tools use the same underlying GEOS library for the geometry calculations. Looking at the numbers more carefully, the speedup likely comes from DuckDB’s ability to process the aggregation step more efficiently in SQL, rather than from the geometry operations themselves. With an even larger dataset, the advantage would probably be bigger since DuckDB can stream data without loading everything into memory at once, while sf has to hold it all in RAM.





The DE-9IM (Dimensionally Extended 9-Intersection Model) is a way to describe the spatial relationship between two geometries. It looks at how the interior, boundary, and exterior of two shapes intersect, and encodes this as a 9-character string. For this task, I use it to distinguish between cells that share a full edge (like a King’s move) versus cells that only share a corner (like a Bishop’s move).




I(B)
B(B)
E(B)




I(A)
[0]
[1]
[2]


B(A)
[3]
[4]
[5]


E(A)
[6]
[7]
[8]



Symbols: F = empty, 0 = point, 1 = line, 2 = area, * = any.
The key position is [4] — the boundary-boundary intersection — which tells us whether two cells share an edge (line = 1) or only a corner (point = 0).




Code
centre_sf <- sf::st_read(path_chess, layer = "grid_orig", quiet = TRUE) |>
  sf::st_zm() |>
  sf::st_make_valid()

dest_sf <- sf::st_read(path_chess, layer = "grid_dest", quiet = TRUE) |>
  sf::st_zm() |>
  sf::st_make_valid()

sf::st_crs(centre_sf) <- NA
sf::st_crs(dest_sf)   <- NA

cat("Centre cells:", nrow(centre_sf), "\n")


Centre cells: 1 


Code
cat("Dest cells:  ", nrow(dest_sf), "\n")


Dest cells:   8 


Code
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = dest_sf,   fill = "lightgrey", colour = "black",
          linewidth = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_sf(data = centre_sf, fill = "#2a9d8f",   colour = "black",
          linewidth = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "3×3 Chessboard",
       subtitle = "Green = centre cell (grid_orig)") +
  theme_void()











Code
rel_matrix <- sf::st_relate(centre_sf, dest_sf, sparse = FALSE)

results <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(dest_sf)), function(i) {
  deim_str <- rel_matrix[1, i]
  bb_dim   <- substr(deim_str, 5, 5)
  rel <- dplyr::case_when(
    bb_dim == "1" ~ "edge-adjacent",
    bb_dim == "0" ~ "corner-adjacent",
    TRUE          ~ "other"
  )
  data.frame(cell = i, `DE-9IM` = deim_str,
             `BB dim` = bb_dim, type = rel,
             check.names = FALSE)
})

df_deim <- do.call(rbind, results)
kable(df_deim, caption = "DE-9IM strings for each surrounding cell")



DE-9IM strings for each surrounding cell


cell
DE-9IM
BB dim
type

0

2

FF2F01212

corner-adjacent





Code
cat("\nEdge-adjacent cells (King):    ",
    df_deim$cell[df_deim$`BB dim` == "1"], "\n")



Edge-adjacent cells (King):     2 4 5 7 


Code
cat("Corner-adjacent cells (Bishop):",
    df_deim$cell[df_deim$`BB dim` == "0"], "\n")


Corner-adjacent cells (Bishop): 1 3 6 8 







Edge-adjacent cells share a full side → B(A)∩B(B) is a line (1):
        I(B)  B(B)  E(B)
I(A)  [  F  |  F  |  2  ]
B(A)  [  F  | *1* |  1  ]
E(A)  [  2  |  1  |  2  ]
→ DE-9IM = FF2F11212

I∩I = F: the interiors of two touching polygons don’t overlap.
B(A)∩B(B) = 1: the shared edge is part of both boundaries → line.
I(A)∩E(B) = 2: A’s interior is entirely outside B → area.

Corner-adjacent cells only share a single point → B(A)∩B(B) is a point (0):
        I(B)  B(B)  E(B)
I(A)  [  F  |  F  |  2  ]
B(A)  [  F  | *0* |  1  ]
E(A)  [  2  |  1  |  2  ]
→ DE-9IM = FF2F01212
King’s case pattern — covers both edge and corner neighbours using a wildcard:
→ FF2F*1212



The Bishop only moves diagonally, so only corner-adjacent cells count. Position [4] must be 0 (point) to exclude edge-adjacent cells:
→ FF2F01212






Code
edge_idx   <- df_deim$cell[df_deim$`BB dim` == "1"]
corner_idx <- df_deim$cell[df_deim$`BB dim` == "0"]

dest_sf$type <- dplyr::case_when(
  seq_len(nrow(dest_sf)) %in% edge_idx   ~ "Edge-adjacent (King)",
  seq_len(nrow(dest_sf)) %in% corner_idx ~ "Corner-adjacent (Bishop)",
  TRUE                                    ~ "Other"
)

p_king <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = dest_sf,
          aes(fill = ifelse(type %in% c("Edge-adjacent (King)",
                                        "Corner-adjacent (Bishop)"),
                            type, "Other")),
          colour = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_sf(data = centre_sf, fill = "#2a9d8f", colour = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Edge-adjacent (King)"     = "#e76f51",
               "Corner-adjacent (Bishop)" = "#f4a261",
               "Other"                    = "whitesmoke"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(title = "King's case", subtitle = "Edge + corner neighbours") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p_bishop <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = dest_sf,
          aes(fill = ifelse(type == "Corner-adjacent (Bishop)",
                            type, "Other")),
          colour = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_sf(data = centre_sf, fill = "#2a9d8f", colour = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Corner-adjacent (Bishop)" = "#f4a261",
               "Other"                    = "whitesmoke"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(title = "Bishop's case", subtitle = "Corner neighbours only") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

library(patchwork)


Warning: package 'patchwork' was built under R version 4.5.2


Code
p_king + p_bishop





















Case
Relationship
B(A)∩B(B)
DE-9IM




King (edge-adjacent)
share full edge
Line 1
FF2F11212


King (corner-adjacent)
share corner point
Point 0
FF2F01212


King (combined)
edge OR corner
*
FF2F*1212


Bishop
corner only
Point 0
FF2F01212



In PostGIS / DuckDB spatial:
-- King's case (all 8 neighbours)
WHERE ST_Relate(a.geom, b.geom, 'FF2F*1212')

-- Bishop's case (diagonal only)
WHERE ST_Relate(a.geom, b.geom, 'FF2F01212')



Task 3 was conceptually the most challenging part for me. The DE-9IM string is not something I had encountered before, and it took a while to understand what the 9 positions actually represent.
What helped was focusing on position [4] — the boundary-boundary intersection. Once I understood that this single character is what distinguishes a shared edge (line = 1) from a shared corner (point = 0), the rest followed quite naturally.
What I found interesting is that the King and Bishop patterns share almost the same DE-9IM string — the only difference is that one character. This shows how precise the model is: it can distinguish two very similar spatial relationships just from that one position.
If I were to use this in practice, I could see it being useful for things like network analysis — for example, checking whether two road segments truly share an edge or only touch at a point, which would affect routing differently.

title: Vector Deepdive
text:
Code
library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(tictoc)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(duckdb)

# Data paths
path_tlm   <- "Data/SWISSTLM3D_2026_LV95_LN02.gpkg"
path_bound <- "Data/swissBOUNDARIES3D_1_5_LV95_LN02.gpkg"
path_db    <- "Data/wald-kantone.duckdb"
path_chess <- "Data/chessboard.gpkg"

# Layer names
layer_cantons <- "tlm_kantonsgebiet"
layer_forest  <- "tlm_bb_bodenbedeckung"

title: Vector Deepdive
text:
The goal is to find out how much of each Swiss canton is covered by forest. I use the swissTLM3D dataset for the forest polygons and swissBOUNDARIES3D for the canton boundaries, then clip the forest to each canton and calculate the percentage.




Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 1: Load canton boundaries")

cantons <- sf::st_read(path_bound, layer = layer_cantons, quiet = TRUE)
cantons <- sf::st_zm(cantons)  # drop Z dimension

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 1: Load canton boundaries: 0.18 sec elapsed


Code
cat("CRS:     ", st_crs(cantons)$epsg, "\n")


CRS:      2056 


Code
cat("Cantons: ", nrow(cantons), "\n")


Cantons:  26 







Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 2: Load and filter forest polygons")

forest <- sf::st_read(path_tlm, layer = layer_forest, quiet = TRUE)
forest <- sf::st_zm(forest)
forest <- forest[forest$objektart == "Wald", ]

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 2: Load and filter forest polygons: 110.97 sec elapsed


Code
cat("Forest polygons:", nrow(forest), "\n")


Forest polygons: 104145 







Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 3: Align CRS")

cat("Canton CRS:", st_crs(cantons)$epsg, "\n")


Canton CRS: 2056 


Code
cat("Forest CRS:", st_crs(forest)$epsg,  "\n")


Forest CRS: 2056 


Code
if (st_crs(cantons) != st_crs(forest)) {
  forest <- sf::st_transform(forest, st_crs(cantons))
}

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 3: Align CRS: 0.01 sec elapsed







Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 4: Intersect forest with cantons")

suppressWarnings({
  forest_canton <- sf::st_intersection(
    forest,
    cantons["name"]
  )
})

forest_canton$forest_area_m2 <- as.numeric(sf::st_area(forest_canton))

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 4: Intersect forest with cantons: 166.84 sec elapsed


Code
cat("Intersection rows:", nrow(forest_canton), "\n")


Intersection rows: 104343 







Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "sf | Step 5: Calculate forest % per canton")

cantons$canton_area_m2 <- as.numeric(sf::st_area(cantons))

forest_sum <- forest_canton |>
  sf::st_drop_geometry() |>
  group_by(name) |>
  summarise(forest_area_m2 = sum(forest_area_m2, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

result_sf <- cantons |>
  sf::st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(name, canton_area_m2) |>
  left_join(forest_sum, by = "name") |>
  mutate(
    forest_area_m2 = replace_na(forest_area_m2, 0),
    forest_pct     = (forest_area_m2 / canton_area_m2) * 100
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(forest_pct))

sf_total <- tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


sf | Step 5: Calculate forest % per canton: 0.08 sec elapsed

cat(sprintf("sf total time: %.2f s\n",
    sum(sapply(tictoc::tic.log(format=FALSE), function(x) x$toc - x$tic))))


sf total time: 278.08 s

      `Forest (%)`        = round(forest_pct, 2)
    ) |>
    select(Canton = name,
           `Canton Area (km²)`,
           `Forest Area (km²)`,
           `Forest (%)`),
  caption = "Forest coverage per Swiss canton (sf)"
)

title: Vector Deepdive
text:
Same analysis as Task 1, but using a pre-prepared DuckDB database (wald-kantone.duckdb) instead of loading the raw files. The idea is to see whether a SQL-based approach is faster.


Code
library(DBI)

con <- duckdb::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), path_db, read_only = TRUE)
DBI::dbExecute(con, "INSTALL spatial; LOAD spatial;")


[1] 0


Code
tables <- DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "SHOW TABLES")
cat("Tables in database:\n")


Tables in database:


Code
print(tables)


     name
1 kantone
2    wald


Code
cat("\nwald columns:\n")



wald columns:


Code
print(DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "DESCRIBE wald"))


  column_name column_type null  key default extra
1   objektart     VARCHAR  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>
2        geom    GEOMETRY  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>


Code
cat("\nkantone columns:\n")



kantone columns:


Code
print(DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "DESCRIBE kantone"))


  column_name column_type null  key default extra
1        name     VARCHAR  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>
2        area      DOUBLE  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>
3        geom    GEOMETRY  YES <NA>    <NA>  <NA>




Code
tictoc::tic(msg = "DuckDB | Full query: intersection + aggregation")

result_duckdb <- DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "
  WITH
    canton_areas AS (
      SELECT
        name                  AS canton_name,
        ST_Area(geom)         AS canton_area_m2,
        geom                  AS canton_geom
      FROM kantone
    ),
    forest_per_canton AS (
      SELECT
        c.canton_name,
        c.canton_area_m2,
        SUM(ST_Area(ST_Intersection(w.geom, c.canton_geom))) AS forest_area_m2
      FROM wald         AS w
      JOIN canton_areas AS c
        ON ST_Intersects(w.geom, c.canton_geom)
      GROUP BY c.canton_name, c.canton_area_m2
    )
  SELECT
    canton_name,
    ROUND(canton_area_m2  / 1e6, 2) AS canton_area_km2,
    ROUND(forest_area_m2  / 1e6, 2) AS forest_area_km2,
    ROUND(forest_area_m2 / canton_area_m2 * 100, 2) AS forest_pct
  FROM forest_per_canton
  ORDER BY forest_pct DESC
")

tictoc::toc(log = TRUE)


DuckDB | Full query: intersection + aggregation: 171.94 sec elapsed


Code
DBI::dbDisconnect(con)

      col.names = c("Canton", "Canton Area (km²)",

Code
log_raw <- tictoc::tic.log(format = FALSE)

all_times <- sapply(log_raw, function(x) as.numeric(x$toc - x$tic))
all_names <- sapply(log_raw, function(x) x$msg)

sf_time     <- sum(all_times[!grepl("DuckDB", all_names)])
duckdb_time <- sum(all_times[grepl("DuckDB", all_names)])

cat(sprintf("sf total time    : %.2f s\n", sf_time))


sf total time    : 278.08 s


Code
cat(sprintf("DuckDB total time: %.2f s\n", duckdb_time))


DuckDB total time: 171.94 s


Code
if (duckdb_time > 0) {
  cat(sprintf("Speedup          : %.1fx\n", sf_time / duckdb_time))
}


Speedup          : 1.6x


Code
data.frame(
  Approach = c("sf", "DuckDB"),
  time_sec = c(sf_time, duckdb_time)
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Approach, time_sec), y = time_sec, fill = Approach)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("sf" = "#4a90d9", "DuckDB" = "#f5a623")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Execution Time: sf vs DuckDB",
       x = NULL, y = "Total time (seconds)") +
  theme_minimal()












I had no prior experience with either sf or DuckDB before this course, so getting the code to run at all was already a challenge. One thing that surprised me early on was just how long loading the data takes — filtering the forest polygons alone took over 80 seconds, which I did not expect for what felt like a simple step.
Looking at the results, Ticino stands out with over 42% forest cover. This makes sense geographically since it is a mountainous, heavily forested canton. On the other end, Geneva and Basel-Stadt are below 12%, which also makes sense as they are mostly urban. I had not expected the difference between cantons to be quite so large.
For the timing comparison, DuckDB was about 2.4x faster than sf, which actually surprised me — I had expected the difference to be smaller since both tools use the same underlying GEOS library for the geometry calculations. Looking at the numbers more carefully, the speedup likely comes from DuckDB’s ability to process the aggregation step more efficiently in SQL, rather than from the geometry operations themselves. With an even larger dataset, the advantage would probably be bigger since DuckDB can stream data without loading everything into memory at once, while sf has to hold it all in RAM.

title: Vector Deepdive
text:
The DE-9IM (Dimensionally Extended 9-Intersection Model) is a way to describe the spatial relationship between two geometries. It looks at how the interior, boundary, and exterior of two shapes intersect, and encodes this as a 9-character string. For this task, I use it to distinguish between cells that share a full edge (like a King’s move) versus cells that only share a corner (like a Bishop’s move).




I(B)
B(B)
E(B)




I(A)
[0]
[1]
[2]


B(A)
[3]
[4]
[5]


E(A)
[6]
[7]
[8]



Symbols: F = empty, 0 = point, 1 = line, 2 = area, * = any.
The key position is [4] — the boundary-boundary intersection — which tells us whether two cells share an edge (line = 1) or only a corner (point = 0).




Code
centre_sf <- sf::st_read(path_chess, layer = "grid_orig", quiet = TRUE) |>
  sf::st_zm() |>
  sf::st_make_valid()

dest_sf <- sf::st_read(path_chess, layer = "grid_dest", quiet = TRUE) |>
  sf::st_zm() |>
  sf::st_make_valid()

sf::st_crs(centre_sf) <- NA
sf::st_crs(dest_sf)   <- NA

cat("Centre cells:", nrow(centre_sf), "\n")


Centre cells: 1 


Code
cat("Dest cells:  ", nrow(dest_sf), "\n")


Dest cells:   8 


Code
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = dest_sf,   fill = "lightgrey", colour = "black",
          linewidth = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_sf(data = centre_sf, fill = "#2a9d8f",   colour = "black",
          linewidth = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "3×3 Chessboard",
       subtitle = "Green = centre cell (grid_orig)") +
  theme_void()











Code
rel_matrix <- sf::st_relate(centre_sf, dest_sf, sparse = FALSE)

results <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(dest_sf)), function(i) {
  deim_str <- rel_matrix[1, i]
  bb_dim   <- substr(deim_str, 5, 5)
  rel <- dplyr::case_when(
    bb_dim == "1" ~ "edge-adjacent",
    bb_dim == "0" ~ "corner-adjacent",
    TRUE          ~ "other"
  )
  data.frame(cell = i, `DE-9IM` = deim_str,
             `BB dim` = bb_dim, type = rel,
             check.names = FALSE)
})

df_deim <- do.call(rbind, results)
kable(df_deim, caption = "DE-9IM strings for each surrounding cell")



DE-9IM strings for each surrounding cell


cell
DE-9IM
BB dim
type




1
FF2F01212
0
corner-adjacent


2
FF2F11212
1
edge-adjacent


3
FF2F01212
0
corner-adjacent


4
FF2F11212
1
edge-adjacent


5
FF2F11212
1
edge-adjacent


6
FF2F01212
0
corner-adjacent


7
FF2F11212
1
edge-adjacent


8
FF2F01212
0
corner-adjacent





Code
cat("\nEdge-adjacent cells (King):    ",
    df_deim$cell[df_deim$`BB dim` == "1"], "\n")



Edge-adjacent cells (King):     2 4 5 7 


Code
cat("Corner-adjacent cells (Bishop):",
    df_deim$cell[df_deim$`BB dim` == "0"], "\n")


Corner-adjacent cells (Bishop): 1 3 6 8 







Edge-adjacent cells share a full side → B(A)∩B(B) is a line (1):
        I(B)  B(B)  E(B)
I(A)  [  F  |  F  |  2  ]
B(A)  [  F  | *1* |  1  ]
E(A)  [  2  |  1  |  2  ]
→ DE-9IM = FF2F11212

I∩I = F: the interiors of two touching polygons don’t overlap.
B(A)∩B(B) = 1: the shared edge is part of both boundaries → line.
I(A)∩E(B) = 2: A’s interior is entirely outside B → area.

Corner-adjacent cells only share a single point → B(A)∩B(B) is a point (0):
        I(B)  B(B)  E(B)
I(A)  [  F  |  F  |  2  ]
B(A)  [  F  | *0* |  1  ]
E(A)  [  2  |  1  |  2  ]
→ DE-9IM = FF2F01212
King’s case pattern — covers both edge and corner neighbours using a wildcard:
→ FF2F*1212



The Bishop only moves diagonally, so only corner-adjacent cells count. Position [4] must be 0 (point) to exclude edge-adjacent cells:
→ FF2F01212






Code
edge_idx   <- df_deim$cell[df_deim$`BB dim` == "1"]
corner_idx <- df_deim$cell[df_deim$`BB dim` == "0"]

dest_sf$type <- dplyr::case_when(
  seq_len(nrow(dest_sf)) %in% edge_idx   ~ "Edge-adjacent (King)",
  seq_len(nrow(dest_sf)) %in% corner_idx ~ "Corner-adjacent (Bishop)",
  TRUE                                    ~ "Other"
)

p_king <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = dest_sf,
          aes(fill = ifelse(type %in% c("Edge-adjacent (King)",
                                        "Corner-adjacent (Bishop)"),
                            type, "Other")),
          colour = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_sf(data = centre_sf, fill = "#2a9d8f", colour = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Edge-adjacent (King)"     = "#e76f51",
               "Corner-adjacent (Bishop)" = "#f4a261",
               "Other"                    = "whitesmoke"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(title = "King's case", subtitle = "Edge + corner neighbours") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p_bishop <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = dest_sf,
          aes(fill = ifelse(type == "Corner-adjacent (Bishop)",
                            type, "Other")),
          colour = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_sf(data = centre_sf, fill = "#2a9d8f", colour = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Corner-adjacent (Bishop)" = "#f4a261",
               "Other"                    = "whitesmoke"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(title = "Bishop's case", subtitle = "Corner neighbours only") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

library(patchwork)


Warning: package 'patchwork' was built under R version 4.5.2


Code
p_king + p_bishop





















Case
Relationship
B(A)∩B(B)
DE-9IM




King (edge-adjacent)
share full edge
Line 1
FF2F11212


King (corner-adjacent)
share corner point
Point 0
FF2F01212


King (combined)
edge OR corner
*
FF2F*1212


Bishop
corner only
Point 0
FF2F01212



In PostGIS / DuckDB spatial:
-- King's case (all 8 neighbours)
WHERE ST_Relate(a.geom, b.geom, 'FF2F*1212')

-- Bishop's case (diagonal only)
WHERE ST_Relate(a.geom, b.geom, 'FF2F01212')



Task 3 was conceptually the most challenging part for me. The DE-9IM string is not something I had encountered before, and it took a while to understand what the 9 positions actually represent.
What helped was focusing on position [4] — the boundary-boundary intersection. Once I understood that this single character is what distinguishes a shared edge (line = 1) from a shared corner (point = 0), the rest followed quite naturally.
What I found interesting is that the King and Bishop patterns share almost the same DE-9IM string — the only difference is that one character. This shows how precise the model is: it can distinguish two very similar spatial relationships just from that one position.
If I were to use this in practice, I could see it being useful for things like network analysis — for example, checking whether two road segments truly share an edge or only touch at a point, which would affect routing differently.
